questions.question: Explain the classification of angles based on their measures with examples.
questions.answer: Angles can be classified as acute (<90°), right (=90°), obtuse (between 90° and 180°), straight (=180°), and reflex (between 180° and 360°).

questions.question: Explain complementary angles with examples and applications.
questions.answer: Complementary angles are two angles whose sum is 90°. For example, 30° and 60° are complementary.

questions.question: Explain supplementary angles with examples.
questions.answer: Supplementary angles are two angles whose sum is 180°. Example: 100° and 80°.

questions.question: Explain the concept of adjacent angles with examples.
questions.answer: Adjacent angles share a common vertex and arm but do not overlap.

questions.question: Explain the linear pair axiom with a suitable example.
questions.answer: The linear pair axiom states that when a ray stands on a line, the sum of the adjacent angles formed is 180°.

questions.question: Explain vertically opposite angles with a diagram description.
questions.answer: When two lines intersect, opposite angles are equal and called vertically opposite angles.

questions.question: Explain the theorem of vertically opposite angles.
questions.answer: When two lines intersect each other, vertically opposite angles are equal.

questions.question: Explain parallel lines with examples.
questions.answer: Parallel lines are lines that never intersect and remain at constant distance from each other.

questions.question: Explain transversal lines and their importance.
questions.answer: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more lines at distinct points and forms various angle pairs.

questions.question: Explain corresponding angles with an example.
questions.answer: Corresponding angles are angles that occupy the same relative position when a transversal cuts two lines.

questions.question: Explain alternate interior angles.
questions.answer: Alternate interior angles lie between two lines and on opposite sides of a transversal.

questions.question: Explain interior angles on the same side of a transversal.
questions.answer: These angles lie inside the two lines and on the same side of the transversal and are supplementary.

questions.question: Explain the sum of angles around a point with example.
questions.answer: The sum of all angles around a point equals 360°.

questions.question: Explain reflex angles with examples.
questions.answer: Reflex angles measure more than 180° but less than 360°.

questions.question: Explain straight angles with examples.
questions.answer: Straight angles measure exactly 180° and form a straight line.

questions.question: Explain right angles with examples.
questions.answer: Right angles measure exactly 90°.

questions.question: Explain acute angles with examples.
questions.answer: Acute angles measure less than 90°.

questions.question: Explain obtuse angles with examples.
questions.answer: Obtuse angles measure between 90° and 180°.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between linear pair and supplementary angles.
questions.answer: Angles forming a linear pair always add up to 180°.

questions.question: Explain why vertically opposite angles are equal.
questions.answer: Using linear pair relationships, vertically opposite angles can be proven equal.

questions.question: Explain properties of parallel lines cut by a transversal.
questions.answer: Corresponding angles and alternate interior angles are equal.

questions.question: Explain how angles are used in geometry.
questions.answer: Angles help measure turns, shapes, and relationships between lines.

questions.question: Explain the role of angles in triangles and polygons.
questions.answer: Angles determine the shape and properties of triangles and polygons.

questions.question: Explain angle measurement using degrees.
questions.answer: Angles are measured in degrees using instruments like protractors.

questions.question: Explain why angles are important in daily life.
questions.answer: Angles are used in construction, design, navigation and engineering.
